questions.specification:
Ecologists monitored the development of an ecosystem on a newly formed volcanic island over a period of 150 years. The study tracked changes in biomass and energy flow as the community underwent primary succession.

| Stage | Years since formation | Total Biomass ($kg\ m^{-2}$) | Gross Primary Productivity ($g\ m^{-2}\ yr^{-1}$) | Respiration ($g\ m^{-2}\ yr^{-1}$) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Pioneer | 10 | 0.2 | 150 | 90 |
| Intermediate | 50 | 4.5 | 800 | 450 |
| Climax | 150 | 18.2 | 1200 | 1150 |

labeledSubSubTopics.path: D - Continuity and Change > D4.2 Sustainability and change > D4.2.13 Changes occurring during primary succession (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: D4.2.13 Changes occurring during primary succession (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question is explicitly about primary succession on a newly formed volcanic island, tracking biomass changes across pioneer/intermediate/climax stages and asking about how conditions change during succession.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C - Interaction and Interdependence > C4.2 Transfer of energy and matter > C4.2.15 Primary production as accumulation of carbon compounds in biomass by autotrophs
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C4.2.15 Primary production as accumulation of carbon compounds in biomass by autotrophs
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Calculating NPP from GPP and respiration and discussing changing energy storage as climax is approached directly tests primary production/biomass accumulation concepts.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: D - Continuity and Change > D4.2 Sustainability and change > D4.2.15 Climax communities and arrested succession (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: D4.2.15 Climax communities and arrested succession (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: References to the ecosystem approaching a climax community and comparison of energy flow/respiration in the climax stage align with climax community characteristics.

parts.content: State the relationship between time and total biomass during this process of primary succession.
parts.markscheme:
- Total biomass increases with time during succession (positive correlation). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Biomass is lowest in the pioneer stage and highest in the climax stage (increasing trend across stages). <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Calculate the net primary productivity (NPP) for the intermediate stage. Then discuss how energy storage (biomass accumulation/NPP) changes from the intermediate stage to the climax community.
parts.markscheme:
- NPP calculation: $\text{NPP} = \text{GPP} - R = 800 - 450 = 350\ g\ m^{-2}\ yr^{-1}$. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Correct units stated for NPP: $g\ m^{-2}\ yr^{-1}$. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- From intermediate to climax, respiration increases substantially (higher community maintenance costs). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- In the climax stage, $\text{GPP} \approx R$ (e.g. $1200$ vs $1150$), so NPP is low and little additional energy is stored as new biomass. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Biomass is still high in the climax community because it has accumulated over time, even though net biomass gain per year is small. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how pioneer species, such as lichens and mosses, alter the abiotic environment to allow for the establishment of later successional stages.
parts.markscheme:
- Physical and/or chemical weathering: pioneer organisms break down bare rock to produce smaller particles. <marks>1 mark</marks>
_**OR**_
- Organic matter/humus addition: death and decomposition of pioneers adds organic material, improving water retention and soil structure. <marks>1 mark</marks>
_**OR**_
- Nutrient enrichment: some pioneers (e.g. cyanobacteria in lichens) fix nitrogen, increasing soil fertility. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Award any two of the above points. <marks>2 marks</marks>

questions.specification:
The table below provides demographic data for a population of prairie dogs observed over a one-year period. At the start of the study, the initial population size was $2,000$.

| Demographic Factor | Number of Individuals |
| :--- | :--- |
| Natality | $420$ |
| Mortality | $310$ |
| Immigration | $70$ |
| Emigration | $140$ |

Based on this data, what is the total population size at the conclusion of the year?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C - Interaction and Interdependence > C4.1 Populations and communities > C4.1.1 Populations
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C4.1.1 Populations
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question requires calculating the change in population size over time based on the four fundamental demographic factors: natality, mortality, immigration, and emigration. This falls directly under the study of Populations (C4.1.1).

options.markscheme: This value represents the initial population minus only the total losses ($2,000 - 310 - 140$), incorrectly ignoring the population gains from natality and immigration.

The final population size is determined by adding the net change (gains minus losses) to the initial population size.

$\text{Net Change} = (\text{Natality} + \text{Immigration}) - (\text{Mortality} + \text{Emigration})$

$\text{Net Change} = (420 + 70) - (310 + 140) = 490 - 450 = +40$

$\text{Final Population} = 2,000 + 40 = 2,040$
This value only accounts for the difference between natality and mortality ($2,000 + 420 - 310$), omitting the combined effects of immigration and emigration on the total population.
This value represents the initial population plus all demographic gains ($2,000 + 420 + 70$) but fails to subtract the individuals lost through mortality and emigration.

The genome size represents the total amount of DNA in a haploid set of chromosomes, while the gene number refers to the total count of protein-coding sequences. The Human Genome Project (HGP) provided the first comprehensive map of the human genome, enabling more precise studies of genetic variation and its molecular consequences.

parts.markscheme:
Award marks for any two appropriate points, one similarity and one difference. 

**Similarity (any one):**
- In both, larger genome size is associated with a higher number of protein-coding genes (compared with the virus shown). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Both have far more genes than T2 phage, consistent with their larger genomes. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Using the data provided and your own knowledge, discuss the relationship between genome size, gene number, and the complexity of an organism.
parts.markscheme:
a. Generally, eukaryotes (*Homo sapiens* / *Paris japonica*) have larger genomes and more genes than prokaryotes (*E. coli*) or viruses (T2 phage). <marks>1 mark</marks>

b. Genome size does not always correlate with the number of genes (e.g., *Paris japonica* has a much larger genome than humans but only slightly more genes). <marks>1 mark</marks>

c. Genome size is not a reliable indicator of organismal complexity (C-value paradox). <marks>1 mark</marks>

d. For example, *Paris japonica* (a plant) has a significantly larger genome than *Homo sapiens*, yet is not considered more complex. <marks>1 mark</marks>

e. Large genomes in some eukaryotes are often due to repetitive / non-coding DNA rather than more functional genes. <marks>1 mark</marks>

f. Gene number is a better, though still imperfect, predictor of complexity than total DNA content. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*[4 max]*

The solid line represents the species' performance when grown in a community alongside several other competing plant species.

![Plant Growth Graph](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-physics-new-a2ebc106-b2c4-400b-b9ca-8aaea2939c35-1771929956947/7e5036b8-2cd0-4d74-811b-4ca3cb0ff347.jpeg)

What does the solid line represent for this species?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: B - Form and Function > B4.2 Ecological niches > B4.2.12 Fundamental and realized niches
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B4.2.12 Fundamental and realized niches
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question specifically asks to distinguish between the performance of a species in isolation (fundamental niche) versus its performance in a community with competition (realized niche), which is the core concept of topic B4.2.12.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: B - Form and Function > B4.2 Ecological niches > B4.2.13 Competitive exclusion and the uniqueness of ecological niches
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B4.2.13 Competitive exclusion and the uniqueness of ecological niches
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The reduction from the fundamental niche to the realized niche shown in the graph is a result of interspecific competition and the competitive exclusion principle.

options.markscheme: Incorrect. The fundamental niche is the total range of environmental conditions under which a species can survive and reproduce, represented here by the dashed line.
Correct. The realized niche is the actual conditions and resources a species uses after taking into account biotic interactions like competition and predation.
Incorrect. Physiological tolerance limits are typically associated with the fundamental niche (dashed line), not the distribution restricted by competition.
Incorrect. Competitive exclusion is a principle stating that two species cannot occupy the same niche indefinitely; it does not describe a specific curve on a performance graph.

![Shelford's Law](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-physics-new-ac830eff-4568-4eec-ad0e-bcce37677c9f-1771929956947/ac39d2a8-addc-4b8b-ab37-d769c53e2e98.jpeg)

Which labelled region represents the **zone of intolerance**, where the species cannot survive?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: B - Form and Function > B4.1 Adaptation to environment > B4.1.4 Range of tolerance of a limiting factor
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B4.1.4 Range of tolerance of a limiting factor
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question specifically asks about Shelford’s Law of Tolerance and the identification of the 'zone of physiological stress' within a species' range, which is the direct focus of topic B4.1.4: Range of tolerance of a limiting factor.

options.markscheme: X represents the optimum range where population density is highest and reproductive success is maximized.
Y represents the zone of physiological stress where the abiotic factor is too high or too low for optimal function; individuals survive but reproduction is compromised.
Z represents the zone of intolerance where the species cannot survive.
X is the optimum range, whereas Y is the zone of physiological stress; neither is the zone of intolerance.

questions.specification:
A team of ecologists surveyed a coastal grassland ecosystem using two methods.

For sessile organisms (clumped grass species), they used random quadrat sampling. They placed 20 quadrats ($1 \text{ m}^2$ each) randomly across the area and recorded the following counts per quadrat:

14, 17, 12, 15, 18, 10, 13, 15, 16, 19, 14, 11, 15, 17, 13, 14, 12, 18, 16, 15.

For motile organisms (a local crab species), they used a capture-mark-release-recapture approach. On day 1, they captured and marked 80 crabs. On day 2, they captured 100 crabs, of which 20 were marked.

Finally, the researchers noted that the grassy habitat can only sustain a maximum of approximately 250 individual crabs because of limited burrow space, food availability, and competition.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C - Interaction and Interdependence > C4.1 Populations and communities > C4.1.3 Random quadrat sampling to estimate population size for sessile organisms
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C4.1.3 Random quadrat sampling to estimate population size for sessile organisms
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Parts (a) and (b) use random quadrat sampling data for a sessile grass population, including calculating mean and interpreting variability.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C - Interaction and Interdependence > C4.1 Populations and communities > C4.1.4 Capture–mark–release–recapture and the Lincoln index
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C4.1.4 Capture–mark–release–recapture and the Lincoln index
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Parts (c) and (d) require using the Lincoln index (capture–mark–release–recapture) and stating its assumptions.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C - Interaction and Interdependence > C4.1 Populations and communities > C4.1.5 Carrying capacity and competition for limited resources
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C4.1.5 Carrying capacity and competition for limited resources
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (e) refers to a maximum sustainable crab population (~250), i.e., carrying capacity, and predicts consequences of exceeding it due to resource competition.

parts.content: Calculate the mean number of grass individuals per $1\text{ m}^2$ quadrat from the quadrat sampling data.
parts.markscheme: Mean $= \frac{294}{20} = 14.7$ individuals per $1\text{ m}^2$ quadrat <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Based on the quadrat data, explain what the standard deviation would tell the researchers about this grass population.
parts.markscheme:
- Standard deviation is a measure of spread/variation in the number of grass individuals per quadrat around the mean <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Larger standard deviation indicates greater variability between quadrats (some quadrats have many individuals, others few) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Smaller standard deviation indicates counts are more similar between quadrats (more uniform distribution across the sampled area) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Link to distribution pattern: greater variability is consistent with patchy/clumped distribution; low variability is consistent with a more even distribution <marks>1 mark</marks>

Award up to 2 marks.

parts.content: Using the Lincoln index (Lincoln–Petersen estimator), predict the total population size of the crabs.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify values: marked on day 1 ($M=80$), total caught on day 2 ($C=100$), marked recaptured on day 2 ($R=20$) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Use Lincoln index: $N = \frac{MC}{R}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Substitute: $N = \frac{80 \times 100}{20}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- $N = 400$ crabs (individuals) <marks>1 mark</marks>

Award up to 2 marks.

parts.content: List two major assumptions that must hold true for the Lincoln index estimate to be valid.
parts.markscheme:
Any two of the following: <marks>2 marks</marks>
- No immigration or emigration between samples (closed population)
_**OR**_
- No births or deaths between samples
_**OR**_
- Marked individuals mix randomly back into the population
_**OR**_
- Marking does not affect survival or likelihood of recapture / marks are not lost and are recognised on recapture

parts.content: Suppose the crab population increases beyond 250 individuals. Predict two likely ecological consequences related to resource competition.
parts.markscheme:
Any two of the following: <marks>2 marks</marks>
- Increased intraspecific competition for burrows/food leading to reduced survival (higher mortality)
_**OR**_
- Reduced growth rate and/or reduced reproductive success due to limited resources
_**OR**_
- Depletion of available resources/habitat degradation (e.g., food scarcity, fewer suitable burrow sites)

questions.specification: Photosynthesis has had major impacts on Earth’s environment and is influenced by several environmental factors.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C - Interaction and Interdependence > C1.3 Photosynthesis > C1.3.7 The effects of limiting factors on the rate of photosynthesis
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C1.3.7 The effects of limiting factors on the rate of photosynthesis
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (b) specifically requires an explanation of how temperature, light intensity, and carbon dioxide concentration act as limiting factors on the rate of photosynthesis.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C - Interaction and Interdependence > C1.3 Photosynthesis > C1.3.4 Separation and identification of photosynthetic pigments by chromatography
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C1.3.4 Separation and identification of photosynthetic pigments by chromatography
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (a) directly asks for an outline of the use of chromatography to separate and identify photosynthetic pigments.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C - Interaction and Interdependence > C1.3 Photosynthesis > C1.3.3 Oxygen as a by-product of photosynthesis
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C1.3.3 Oxygen as a by-product of photosynthesis
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (0) asks for the effects of the evolution of photosynthesis on the atmosphere and oceans, which is the direct result of oxygen being produced as a by-product of photosynthesis.

parts.content: Describe the changes in the Earth’s atmosphere and oceans resulting from the evolution of photosynthesis.
parts.markscheme:
- The first photosynthetic organisms were prokaryotes/cyanobacteria <marks>1 mark</marks>
- They produced oxygen ($O_2$) through the photolysis of water <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Oxygen reacted with dissolved iron in the oceans, causing it to precipitate as iron oxide <marks>1 mark</marks>
- This led to the formation of banded iron formations in sedimentary rocks <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Atmospheric oxygen concentrations rose significantly from trace amounts/near zero to approximately $20\%$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- This oxygenation allowed for the development of the ozone layer

_**OR**_

- This oxygenation allowed for the evolution of aerobic respiration <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Outline how chromatography is used to separate and identify photosynthetic pigments.
parts.markscheme:
- Leaf tissue is ground with a solvent (e.g., acetone or propanone) to extract pigments <marks>1 mark</marks>
- A concentrated spot of the extract is placed on the baseline/origin of the chromatography paper or TLC plate <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The paper/plate is placed in a container with a running solvent <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Pigments travel at different speeds based on their solubility in the solvent/affinity for the paper <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The distance moved by the pigment is compared to the solvent front to calculate the $Rf$ value <marks>1 mark</marks>
- $Rf = \frac{\text{distance moved by pigment}}{\text{distance moved by solvent front}}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how temperature, light intensity, and carbon dioxide concentration act as limiting factors of photosynthesis.
parts.markscheme:
- A limiting factor is the factor furthest from its optimum that restricts the rate of a reaction <marks>1 mark</marks>
- As light intensity increases, the rate increases up to a plateau <marks>1 mark</marks>
- At the plateau, light is no longer limiting and another factor (e.g., $CO_2$) is in short supply <marks>1 mark</marks>
- As $CO_2$ concentration increases, the rate increases up to a plateau <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Temperature increases the rate up to an optimum due to increased molecular collisions <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Beyond the optimum temperature, enzymes (e.g., Rubisco) denature and the rate drops sharply <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The rate of photosynthesis can be measured directly by oxygen ($O_2$) production/bubble counting <marks>1 mark</marks>
- ...or by the rate of carbon dioxide ($CO_2$) uptake/pH change in water <marks>1 mark</marks>
- ...or indirectly by the increase in dry biomass of the plant <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Up to one additional mark for an accurate detail from AHL (e.g., mention of the light-independent stage or carboxylation)</note>

labeledSubSubTopics.title: A2.3.6  Rapid evolution in viruses (HL)

parts.content: Describe the effect of antigen variability on vaccine effectiveness.
parts.markscheme:
- Antigen variability refers to changes in the pathogen’s antigen molecules/epitopes that are recognized by the immune system <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Vaccines stimulate production of specific antibodies and memory cells to those antigens <marks>1 mark</marks>
- If antigens change, existing antibodies/memory cells bind less well or do not recognize the new antigen, so the secondary response is less effective (reduced immunity/protection) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- As a result vaccine effectiveness decreases and updated vaccines/booster doses may be needed to match circulating variants <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Antigen changes can arise by mutation/recombination leading to new strains/variants that escape immunity <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Even with variability, some protection may remain if conserved epitopes are shared (partial cross-immunity) <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification:
Ecologists investigated the distribution of two species of grasses, *Agrostis tenuis* and *Festuca ovina*, in a prairie ecosystem. After sampling 100 quadrats, they performed a chi-squared test to determine if there was an association between the species. The calculated chi-squared ($$\chi^2$$) value was $$1.14$$, while the critical value at the $$5\%$$ significance level was $$3.84$$. 

Which of the following is the most appropriate conclusion for this investigation?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C - Interaction and Interdependence > C4.1 Populations and communities > C4.1.15 Use of the chi-squared test for association between two species
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C4.1.15 Use of the chi-squared test for association between two species
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question specifically involves interpreting a chi-squared test used to investigate the association between two species in a community, which is the exact subject of this leaf-level topic.

options.content: There is a significant positive association between the species.
The null hypothesis must be rejected.
The two species are distributed independently of each other.
The species are significantly less likely to be found together than expected.
